def add_word(self, word, freq=None, tag=None):
        """
        Add a word to dictionary.

        freq and tag can be omitted, freq defaults to be a calculated value
        that ensures the word can be cut out.
        """
        self.check_initialized()
        word = strdecode(word)
        freq = int(freq) if freq is not None else self.suggest_freq(word, False)
        #if word in default dict then update it using user_dict 
        if word in self.FREQ:
            old_freq = self.FREQ[word]
            self.FREQ[word] = freq
            self.total = self.total - old_freq + freq
        else:
            self.FREQ[word] = freq 
            self.total += freq
        if tag:
            self.user_word_tag_tab[word] = tag
        for ch in xrange(len(word)):
            wfrag = word[:ch + 1]
            if wfrag not in self.FREQ:
                self.FREQ[wfrag] = 0
        if freq == 0:
            finalseg.add_force_split(word)

The way OpenStack consumers are using retrying today (and soon tenacity I hope) can be generalized as:
- Retry some function that performs actions(s). In this case the retried function retries if its action(s) raise an exception. If this times out retrying, consumers expect to get the last raised exception.
- Retry some function gated on a condition(s). In this case the retried function retries until some predicate(s) is True. If it times out retrying/tenacity framework will raise the ``RetryError``. In this case consumers explicitly catch the RetryError and take action (ex: https://github.com/openstack/ironic/blob/master/ironic/drivers/modules/ssh.py#L512).

What I'm proposing is some way to maintain this behavior in tenacity.

While I think the ``reraise()`` you proposed above is a great start, I was also hoping to see some way to specify reraise should be used on failure. If we don't have this "switch" then all consumers in the 1st case above (the most common case) will still need to add a new except block to ``reraise()``.

For example https://github.com/openstack/ironic/blob/master/ironic/conductor/task_manager.py#L255 will have to be rewritten as:
```py
        try:
            reserve_node()
        except tenacity.RetryError as retry_err:
            retry_err.reraise()
```

While this is a minor change, it'll be used in almost all our existing consumers that fall into case 1 above. It would be much less impact to consumers if they could just do:

```py
@retry(reraise_by_default=True,...)
def reserve_node(...):
```

to signify they want tenacity to reraise for them; no other changes needed. If for some reason the last exception is a RetryError, then it's just reraised.

BTW, because vext links from your virtualenv back to the system python, it will only work with the system python used to create the virtualenv (because the python version must be identical).

- Basically there is a file called 'orig-prefix.txt' in the venv, that has a line that points at the system python.

Vext then looks there for QT5.


If things are working properly you should get similar output from these diagnostic commands:
```
$ vext -l
Running in virtualenv [enabled]
pyqt5.vext

```
$ vext -c pyqt5.vext
import sip: [success]
import PyQt5: [success]
```

You are using `augment_images()` but only provide one image, probably of shape (H,W,C). The function then thinks that you are providing H images of shape (W,C) (i.e. 2d images), augments each one on its own and returns a completely broken result. Use `augment_image()` (without the s at the end) to augment a single image.
Besides, you blur two times with each having a sigma of up to `5`, which is rather strong.

import timeit

async def task():
    for i in range(1000):
        await curio.sleep(0)

    start = timeit.default_timer()
    async with curio.TaskGroup() as g:
        for i in range(3):

    span = timeit.default_timer() - start

So the issue here is that if asynchronous generators never get run to completion and thus never unwind their stack in an "asynchronous context", they'll get garbage collected and `__del__` will be called, which is a synchronous context, and thus they'll never actually finalise things properly?

Is there a reason curio can't just do this:

```
def __del__(self):
    curio.run(self.clean_up())
```

D'oh. Looks like the Node lib does not suffer from this: https://github.com/hueniverse/hawk/blob/master/lib/utils.js#L147

Firstly, it's difficulty to correctly convert between UTC time and Epoch time because [Epoch time does not count leap seconds](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time).  Consequently it's also difficult to convert between local times and Epoch time.

I still think we should have a function that converts between Epoch time and datetimes because Python already provides `*fromtimestamp` methods on datetime.  But we should be sure to document this difficulty.

Unfortunately the pull request's `datetime_to_epoch` implementation can't convert a timezone aware `datetime` to Epoch time at all!  That's because Python disallows arithmetic between timezone aware and naive `datetime` objects.

It's possible that we can use the current implementation in the event that the datetime is naive, and just assume that it's UTC.  That seems wrong to me, though.

With that in mind I did some  very basic research.  It seems that the best solution involves [`time.mktime`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html#time.mktime). 

It's the inverse of [`time.locatime`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html#time.localtime), though, so it assumes that the incoming `time.struct_time` object represents a moment in the same timezone as the program in which it's called.  

Consequently our `datetime_to_epoch` function should require the `datetime` object be timezone aware, so that it can convert it to local time via [`datetime.datetime.astimezone`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.astimezone).  It also needs the system's local timezone.

Below is a sketch that shows one way to put these ideas together:

````python
from boltons.tzutils import Pacific, Eastern, UTC
import time


def datetime_to_epoch(dt, timezone):
    if not dt.tzinfo:
        raise ValueError('datetime_to_epoch'
                         ' cannot be applied to a naive datetime')
    if dt.tzinfo != timezone:
        dt = dt.astimezone(timezone)

    return time.mktime(dt.timetuple())
````

And here's what it looks like in action:

````python
import datetime
now = datetime.datetime.now().replace(tzinfo=Pacific)
print datetime_to_epoch(now, timezone=Pacific)
print datetime_to_epoch(now, timezone=Eastern)
print datetime_to_epoch(now, timezone=UTC)
````

A nicer API would not require the `timezone` argument, but instead would use a new timezone in boltons along the lines of [dateutil.tz.tzlocal](https://labix.org/python-dateutil#head-5fb12f4538c5a2fd83f87eea8e6c0ddd47f8b4b0).

What about using a varying size bounding box ? Physically & historically, each symbol are separated because else it would become unreadable. I think it's a good idea to compute a bounding box while a symbol is being drawn whose size is adjusted by the current stroke length. When the pen is lifted, one has to assume to either "start a new symbol" or "concatenate existing symbol". This decision can be based on a bounding box whose shape can be trained, but with a size that's dynamic. For example, when drawing the "integral" symbol, the bounding box is an horizontally thin and vertically tall rectangle that would be recognized by the network, but the size itself is extracted from the current symbol's size. If you remove the `#line` directives from the preprocessed file you'll get the coords inside the preprocessed file.  A quick `sed` command could accomplish this.

This would mean any downstream compiler steps would **also** report the preprocessed file coords, which probably isn't what you want.  If your preprocessor output is `file.i`, I would create a new `file.noline.i` with `#line` directives replaced with blank lines (so the line numbers stay the same).  Feed `file.noline.i` into pycparser, then use the output to modify `file.i`.

(Note, though, that if you're adding `#pragma`s to `file.i`, you're changing line offsets, meaning downstream compiler errors will report the wrong line number from the original.  You would need to ensure you only add `#pragma`s onto existing blank lines.)

That's not a bug no. This is how the invocation order is.

As noted in the [migration page](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/migrating.html#before-and-after-invocation-hooks), the invocation order for hooks is as follows:

1. Command local before invocation hook
2. Cog local before invocation hook
3. Global before invocation hook
4. The actual command
5. Command local after invocation hook
6. Cog local after invocation hook
7. Global after invocation hook

Since a group is technically a command then that's the behaviour you're seeing.

If you want specific behaviour you can just use one that invokes in the order you want (such as a cog local one) and then work around it using something like `ctx.command.parent is self.group_command` or whatever.

On the plane back from Madrid, I realized that these estimators are actually just the normal xi+ and xi- for the cross correlation between the halo ellipticities and the sources, where the halo ellipticity is normalized to have |g| = 1 (i.e., it is just the phase g1 + i g2 = exp(i phi)).  

I made a unit test to confirm this, which is now in the code [here](https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/blob/master/tests/test_gg.py#L529) (on master, but it works with the current v3.2.1 release -- no extra source code required).

So the basic code flow is along the lines of the following:
```
    lens_absg = numpy.sqrt(lens_g1**2 + lens_g2**2)
    lens_cat = treecorr.Catalog(ra=lens_ra, dec=lens_dec, g1=lens_g1/lens_absg, g2=lens_g2/lens_absg)
    source_cat = treecorr.Catalog(ra=source_ra, dec=source_dec, g1=source_g1, g2=source_g2, w=source_w)
    gg = treecorr.GGCorrelation(min_sep=1, max_sep=30, bin_size=0.1)
    gg.process(lens_cat, source_cat)
    gamma_4theta = gg.xim
    gamma_const = gg.xip
    gamma_4theta_x = gg.xim_im

We could leave the `pixel_array` property alone for backwards-compatibility, but push a getter and setter (`set_pixel_array()`, `get_pixel_array()`; an internal function already exists for the getter). With an explicit setter we could also pass a parameter, something like `propagate_changes` for lack of a better term. "or could throw an error or warning of possible inconsistent pixel data changes." This seems like a good idea no matter what and could be incorporated into the setter. It seems like a good idea still though to set other relevant tags with the setter as well.

The reason for switching everything to `Relu` is so that I can override Relu's backprop step. 

- Modifying softmax is ok since we want to make it linear while taking gradient anyways. By using relu instead of linear, we are making it ignore negative stimulus, which is what motivated guided backprop in the first place.

- Yes, swap all activations, including advanced with Relu. Again, the reason for doing this is so that backprop behavior can be modified with a single gradient override.

- Yes, they need to be Relu as well. There is no double activation since we create a new model with modified activation. The old model remains the same (the function does not mutate input model).

One more piece of food for thought from a colleague while you're thinking about this:
"I was just having a read through the issues thread on the eppy github page.  It seems that they are concerned about people making improvements to eppy and not contributing it back.  My argument would be that in the vast majority of cases people who make changes to an open source library will actually want their changes merged back into the ‘official’ version.  Otherwise it makes it difficult for them to update to the latest version – e.g. if we modify eppy and then a new version is released, either we have to merge our modifications with the new version (error prone, time consuming) or we can’t update to the new version.  Which is why I think many, many projects are happy with the MIT license."

many major updates were made under the hood to resolve this issue.
- idd_info has knowledge of the groups (idd file classifies the objects into groups)
    - the older code had no knowledge of groups
- each object in the idf file now has access to all other objects in the idf file.
    - in the older code, each idf object was standalone and did not know that that other idf objects existed
- if you remove an idf object from one idf file and insert it nto another idf file, if knows which idf file it belongs to. If you simply remove an idf object from an idf file, it knows that it blongs to no idf file
- Now it is possible for an idf object to scan through it's idf file and find other idf objects that are related to it (thru object-list and reference)   

As a result the following functions now work:
- zone.zonesurfaces -> will return all the surfaces that belong to the zone
- surface.subsurfaces -> will return all the subsurfaces (windows, doors etc.) that belong to the surface

```

`_restriction_dict` can be accessed directly from this object - but this won't be something that is reliable in all cases (e.g., `RestrictedClassType` actually wraps itself sometimes to support inheritance).

It seems to me that using the PyangBind output here is probably not optimal - since I would imagine that you're not creating the table in the database at every run of the code. It would seem more optimal to try and build something that directly parses the YANG to figure out a schema for the database, since YANG itself is describing a schema (albeit one that might not be easily mappable to SQL).

An alternative would be - if using PostgreSQL at least - to simply store a Path,JSON blob output - since the data instance is already validated.

Alright, last minute change of heart.  Fnmatch will not allow `EXTGLOB` and `DOTGLOB`, but will only use the match variants.  We already have the shorthand that can be used for consistency.  Wcmatch will allow `EXTGLOB`, but only because the implementation conflicted with documentation, but it has been deprecated and will be removed in 3.0.0.

Technically, only glob will allow `EXTGLOB` and `DOTGLOB` moving forward as those are the names that Bash uses for glob, but he `MATCH` variants can also be used there for consistency.

In short, `EXTMATCH` and `DOTMATCH` are the consistent flag names, only glob allows `GLOB` variants, but wcmatch will temporarily allow  `GLOB` variants due to a bug when implemented; docs stated `EXTMATCH`, but we were using `EXTGLOB` on accident.

When initially taking the "pywinusb" namespace I started with the pywinusb.hid package, so anyone or myself could extend it or split it on new packages (e.g. pywinusb.pnp).

This never happened, so going to a monolithic package is feasible.

Anyway, using package namespaces was considered a "good practice" when I started this package, and important dependencies you might end up requiring would still be designed to be deployed as a namespace.

Hence, using a binary installer that supports package namespaces is the best way to go (or someone has to provide a package to py2exe to support them).

@bemall I think it's an issue of tree structure. The "app" is in the wrong subtree (at least according to conventional django). The way it's organized is:

- example
  - manage.py
  - example
    - app
      - models.py
      - ...
    - urls.py
    - settings.py
    - templates
        - ...
    - wsgi.py
    - ...

This format isn't really idiomatic, but really shouldn't startle django veterans. The templates folder is configured in settings.py and as long as urls.py and settings.py correctly import the app, it can really exist anywhere, including places out of the source tree. I've never really seen a case where you'd want to nest apps as done in here, but if you did, nesting them in the "main" app is probably the least unusual way... if that makes sense.

If you're contemplating nesting apps, I would urge you to instead keep it as one app and split up the files in the app into a directory. This will take some work, but will be much cleaner in the end. Apps are meant to be siblings in a project, not parents and children -- even if they may depend on each other. There are a number of changes that cause unexpected behavior with nested apps [Including this](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/23348). It's not a supported workflow, as far as I know. Apps can contain modules rather than files if they get too large and are at a size where you might want nested apps. Here is how I would expect to see the django example laid out, in idiomatic django:

- example
  - manage.py
  - templates
      - ...
  - app
    - models.py
    - ...
  - example
    - urls.py
    - settings.py
    - wsgi.py
    - ...

To do this, there would need to be a few pathing changes to urls.py and settings.py, but it should work with a direct copy and paste, otherwise. I don't know that it's a big enough deal to modify the example, however.

@berdon That's odd, we haven't experienced that 401 auth required error.  We have apps with the email requesting both on and off with the latest version of PSA.  Could there be some other factor causing it?

We had that kind of problem as well.

After a long investigation, we found that those errors **should** occur in some of the cases.
When you are using Django, social_auth is saving the `state` secret inside the session object
and comparing it later on to the state been received from the provider.

We found out the some of our users, are sharing the already generated `urls` we providing for the social-auth (ex `https://**provider**login/?next=/accounts/o/authorize/%3Fclient_id=CLIENT_ID&redirect_uri=RETURN_URL&state=GENERAED AND SAVE STATE IN SESSION&response_type=BLA`)

When those users succesffully entered thier authetication details they been redirected back to our site and `AuthStateMissing` exception been raised.

My suggestion is to catch the `AuthStateMissing` exception in a middleware (django) and verified that session object is exists as needed for the state validation check, It it do existing go and dig futher, otherwise your are wasting your time.

> Multi{processing, threading} isn't enough for this particular use-case. Here's a minimal working example for the kind of case where `Current time` would come in handy:

I see your point and I agree rewriting the code can be tricky but I still think it's the better solution for your problem. Here's a (theoretically, untested) version of your code with threads:


```py
import threading
from time import sleep
from random import random
from progressbar import ProgressBar

def some_function():
    sleep(random() if random() > .01 else 1e4)

class Worker(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.i = 0
        super(Worker, self).__init__()

    def run(self):
        for i in list(range(self.n)):
            some_function()

worker = Worker(10000)
bar = ProgressBar(max_value=worker.n)
worker.start()
while worker.is_alive():
    bar.update(worker.i)
    worker.join(0.1)
```

Turns out Windows input is tricky.  The input for Unicode characters pasted into the console doesnt generate key down events, just key up.  However, standard input only guarantees to get key down in the right order and uses key down for repeats.  In an attempt to be future proof, I've therefore taken to tracking which keys are pressed and just assuming random injected key ups need to be processed as new key presses.  See https://github.com/peterbrittain/asciimatics/commit/58a14c7bdb1b60177f49ff2d4b755ffca2cdb8ae for details.

I discovered that this problem also happens on RHEL7 with the standard pyOpenSSL.
Since rhnlib and some other default Red Hat infrastructure requires pyOpenSSL you cannot easily replace it.

And the proposed/merged workaround helps to avoid the exception (and Ansible failing to load winrm when credssp is installed with the old pyOpenSSL), but it does not make it work unless we force it to us TLSv1 with some compatibility work. Not sure if we want that.

So at least Ansible can survive an installed CredSSP and old PyOpenSSL, but it won't actually do CredSSP with the old OpenSSL. (That will still fail)

                                              [1, 2, 3]
{"op": "remove", "path": "/1", "value": 2}    [1, 3]

{"op": "add", "path": "/1", "value": 1}       [3, 1]
{"op": "add", "path": "/2", "value": 4}       [3, 1, 4]
{"op": "add", "path": "/3", "value": 2}       [3, 1, 4, 2]
```
do we know why the second ``remove`` has ``"value": 2``? Shouldn't it be 1?


My hand-made json patch for this change would be
```
                                              [1, 2, 3]
{"op": "move", "from": "/2", "path": "/0"}    [3, 1, 2]

which I arrived at by noticing that 1 and 2 are in the same order in the resultant list. I don't know how I'd put that down programatically (least of all in a nice generic way that would work in all cases).

@fuhrysteve We use this to create JSON Schemas that our iOS team who pipes it straight into his code to do black magic so he can automatically handle our responses. In the past, we used marshmallow and wrote our schemas by hand. These would frequently fall out of sync during development, hence why we started using your library. All of our JSON Schemas end up living in a RAML document with a Jinja helper that @hjc1710 wrote (roping him in here cause I think he'll enjoy it).

On to the issues we have run into:

1. Our iOS guy needs super accurate `required` keys for his responses. This leads to an issue because....
2. ...we often use the same schema to load and dump our data, so we need contextual `required` keys.
3. We are trying **very, very** hard to have this library generate all our JSON Schemas, but it's next to impossible to have some of this stuff describe complex JSON Schema stuff that reflects how a route works (i.e. a complex `anyOf` that describes how a `@validates_schema` works).
4. Nested recursive `required`s are impossible ATM (that's part of the reason why I opened this PR)

We have a fixes for points 1 & 2 that will be in an upcoming PR (we pass a kwarg to each field called either `load_required` or `dump_required` and when init'ing the schema, we pass `dump_schema` or `load_schema` to it so it outputs the proper required keys). Point 3 could easily be accomplished with callback function in the schema itself. Point 4 is just updating how `get_required` works, which will also be in an upcoming PR.

Hello @pvanek because of how django-tables2-column-shifter works (storing selected invisible columns to the localStorage using javascript) you'll need to *also* use javascript to modify the query so that the excluded columns would be passed to Django. I don't think it is possible to implement this using querystring.

For this you can use the ``$.django_tables2_column_shifter_hidden()`` API call to retrieve the hidden column names (see #11), modify the query to pass these columns along with the ``_export=format`` to the backend and finally capture the column names in the backend and pass them to the ``Exporter``. Take a look at my comment on #9 to see a more thorough explanation. 

When I'll find the time I'll provide a complete template + backend for this functionality.

If I would do this, then I'd have the same mask for every iteration / batch. 
This would be equal to corrupt my data directly (outside the net by corruption a fraction of p) and then plug it into the net.
This has the huge disadvantage, that if any observation is drawn more than once, the corruption pattern is guaranteed the same (thus not random) and the net doesn't learn a generalization to fill in the gaps. 

But if the intention is to generate a new corruption mask _every time a new batch is drawn_ this won't work either.

So let's see this from mesos POV - Airflow is just one framework registered with mesos amongst others ( like Marathon, Chronos, Aurora, Spark etc ). When a slave becomes free it notifies the mesos master and the mesos master ( based on its [allocation](http://mesos.apache.org/documentation/latest/allocation-module/) strategy ) then creates resource offers and send them to registered frameworks. 

So in our case airflow will receive resource offers from Mesos master depending upon the allocator and how many resources are free, and then its upto the Airflow's MesosExecutor whether to accept or reject the offer. In its current form, the Mesos Executor has a queue where it has list of scheduled tasks and as it receives offers from Mesos master it checks for resources in that offer and if that resources value matches the expectations in the config then it accepts the offer and launches tasks on that mesos slave via master. Hope this clears it up.

I've thought about this for a while and I think I'm going to pass on it for `.clean()`.

There are two big reasons for doing this:

1. `.clean()` is about removing malicious content--not about transforming HTML documents for other mediums or prettifying content. `.clean()` is a security-focused function and as such, keeping its functionality minimal reduces the likelihood of bugs that have security-related impact. That's really important.
2. Seems like the use cases for stripping content in tags are related to transforming HTML documents and prettifying content. I think that should get handled by a different function or possibly not by bleach at all.

Towards that, I've clarified the goals/non-goals language and updated the docs to make it clearer what `.clean()` is supposed to be doing and what it's not going to be good for.

Given that, I'm going to pass on this and close it out.

I'm game for talking about building a `.prettify()` function which is about prettifying HTML which would cover the use these changes cover. That should be a new issue, though.

But in any case the pointer magic is wrong, because you multiply the index with 4. The intention here seems like you want to increase the pointer by 4 bytes. But since your pointer is a float pointer every increase or decrease occurs in a multiple of sizeof( pointer type aka float in this case )

So when you increase the float pointer by one. The address of the float pointer will increase by 4 bytes
In general every pointer arithmetic will automatically take the size of the pointer type into account.

In case you want to increase your pointer byte wise, you have to case your pointer to a byte sized type like char.

Either way is fine, just use whichever method you feel is more readable. As far as messages not deleting, you will need to call `get_notifications` a second time to delete any particular message. I ran the same code (slightly modified) and messages deleted just fine.


```python
import boto3


def get_notifications():
    sqs = boto3.client('sqs')
    url = 'https://sqs.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/foo/bar'
    MESSAGE_KEY = 'Messages'
    while True:
        response = sqs.receive_message(QueueUrl=url)

        if MESSAGE_KEY not in response:
            continue

        message = response[MESSAGE_KEY][0]
        yield message['Body']

        sqs.delete_message(
            QueueUrl=url, ReceiptHandle=message['ReceiptHandle'])


for message in get_notifications():
    print(message)
```

On the vSphere end the `timezone` field *has* to be an `int` and that would have to map to one of these values [in sysprep](https://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc749073\(v=ws.10\).aspx) and it would seem that the windows sysprep tool doesn't understand UTC. It does understand GMT and GMT is _supposed_ to not observe daylight savings time. The British use the BST time code in the summer if memory serves ... (that means while Greenwich the city might or might not move it's clocks ... because they are a British city ... depending on how the Windows developers interpreted the timezone request you could argue it would be wrong to automatically move the clock of the guest. Or, the windows developers thought they might be helpful to residents of Greenwich by changing their clocks for them.)

Steps:
1. Use the specific github branch:
      pip install git+git://github.com/wildfish/django-star-ratings.git@feature/swapable-rating-model
2. In settings.py, add 'star_ratings' to the INSTALLED_APPS
3. In urls.py, add a rule for star_ratings.
4. In myapp.models.py, extends the AbstractBaseRating class to add an object_id of the type that matches the primary key of your model. Let the name of new class be MyStarRating
5. In settings.py, set STAR_RATINGS_RATING_APP = "myapp.MyStarRating"
6. Now, add the {% load ratings %} to the template and continue with the rest of the stuff as per the documentation.

Something we *would* like to be faster is `pipenv lock --keep-outdated`, where we're only updating the lock file to account for changes in `Pipfile`, not for new releases on PyPI. Even for the plain `pipenv lock` case, it would be nice to avoid the download to recheck the dependencies of any releases which are the same version we already locked last time.

I don't think that implies storing hierarchical dependency metadata, though - we just need a cache of "project-name-and-version" -> "declared-dependencies" metadata, similar to the way the lock file already stores "project-name-and-version" -> "expected-artifact-hashes".

That way when `pipenv` starts a lockfile update operation, it can read the old lock file to prepopulate an internal cache, and only have to resort to the local artifact cache or the index server for *different* versions of packages (wherever those may appear in the overall dependency hierarchy).

Thanks for the detailed write up! I can see this is one of those cases where the metaclass magic we're using to make things "simpler" for people can actually make things more confusing.

`outputs` can be a list of `Stream` objects, or a list of strings.  If it's a list of strings, those are the names of the fields for the `default` stream.

`inputs` can be a list of `Stream` objects, but these are not objects you build yourself, which could definitely use some clarification in the docs.  For what you want to do, the simplest thing would be:

```py
from streamparse import Bolt, Grouping, Spout, Stream, Topology


class FirstSpout(Spout):
    outputs = ['stuff']


class SecondSpout(Spout):
    outputs = ['other_stuff']


class ProcessBolt(Bolt):
    outputs = [Stream(fields=['data'], name='unit'),
               Stream(fields=['data'], name='incident')]


class UnitBolt(Bolt):
    outputs = ['unit_to_save']


class IncidentBolt(Bolt):
    outputs = ['incident_to_save']


class SaveBolt(Bolt):
    outputs = []


class ExampleTopology(Topology):
    first_spout = FirstSpout.spec()
    second_spout = SecondSpout.spec()
    process_bolt = ProcessBolt.spec(inputs=[first_spout, second_spout])
    unit_bolt = UnitBolt.spec(inputs=[process_bolt['unit']])
    incident_bolt = IncidentBolt.spec(inputs=[process_bolt['incident']])
    save_bolt = SaveBolt.spec(inputs=[unit_bolt, incident_bolt])
```

The key here is that to get a specific output `Stream` from a `ComponentSpec`, you just `__getitem__` via `spec['foo']`.

If you wanted to do a fields grouping with `unit_bolt` and `incident_bolt`, you could just do:

```py
    unit_bolt = UnitBolt.spec(inputs={process_bolt['unit']: Grouping.fields('data')})
    incident_bolt = IncidentBolt.spec(inputs={process_bolt['incident']: Grouping.fields('data')})
```

Hope that clears things up.

This is how it should be specified for Elasticsearch:

- [Direct indexing](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-indexer/blob/master/invenio_indexer/api.py#L135):
  ```python
  client.index(...,parent='<id of parent>')
  ```
- [Bulk indexing](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-indexer/blob/master/invenio_indexer/api.py#L285-L293):
  ```python
  {
    '_index': '...',
    '_parent': '<id of parent>',
    ...
  }
  ```

Now in terms of Invenio-Indexer API I see two options:

Option 1) Explicit specify parent
```python
RecordIndexer.index(child_record, parent=parent_record.id)
RecordIndexer.index_by_id(child_record_id, parent=parent_record_id)
RecordIndexer.bulk_index([(child_id, parent_id), ...])
RecordIndexer.bulk_index([child_id, ...]) # backward compatibility 
```

Option 2) Programmatically extract parent from record
```python
RecordIndexer.index(child_record)
RecordIndexer.index_by_id(child_record_id)
RecordIndexer.bulk_index([child_id, ...])
```

This would require that the parent is either stored or programmatically determined from the record (similar to how we determine the index).

2a) Stored in [RecordMetadata](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records/blob/master/invenio_records/models.py#L60)

```python
RecordMetadata:
  id = ..
  json = ..
  version_id = ...
  created = ...
  updated = ...
```

I think a leading namespace prefix convention would be nice, like GNU/Linux is using in commands `useradd`,  `userdel`,  `usermod` (though there is also `adduser`...)

With CLI auto-completion, we could have

- `inveniomanage accounts usercreate`
- `inveniomanage accounts userdelete`
- `inveniomanage accounts rolecreate`
- `inveniomanage accounts roledelete`

etc.  Typing `user TAB`, triggering auto-completion, and seeing all actions that are available to manipulate users, seems useful.

P.S. BTW another naming decision may be words coming in pairs, such as add/remove, create/destroy, with a chain like "create -> add -> remove -> destroy".

Ah, my mistake, the actual issue is that buildozer itself is not compatible with newer SDKs. It's still good that you're set up with p4a master, your clone method is what I'd recommend for working on this, but it isn't the immediate problem

The issue is that, as outlined by the message, newer SDKs replaced the android binary with sdkmanager. That means buildozer's attempt to query the sdk list [here](https://github.com/kivy/buildozer/blob/master/buildozer/targets/android.py#L324) needs to be changed to use sdkmanager, or ideally to use sdkmanager or android depending on what's available.

The correct command is something like `sdkmanager --list`, as has automatically been guessed in your log, but in that case it looks like some java error has occurred. Does running `/home/kivy/Android/Sdk/tools/bin/sdkmanager --list --verbose` work if you do it directly?

@pmlandwehr I know this goes beyond the question you asked; but you
might find it useful...

If the goal is to compute the closest distance of a point P to a
collection C of a `N` points, then you have to do `N` distance
calculations.  However, if you want to compute the closest point in C to
a bunch of `M` points, that you can much better than computing `MN`
distances by putting C into a VP tree.  The cost to construct the VP
tree is `N log N`; subsequently each closest distance calculation takes
`log N`.  So the total cost is `(N + M) log N`.

Lastly, I am interested at looking at implementing the geographiclib
distance function in numpy.  I did a similar "vectorized" implementation
for MATLAB.  Holding me up is the fact that I have no experience with
numpy!

I suspect my try it out quick advice was bad.  The 'venv' in play is a pex chroot built for tests and that uses the pex in 3rdparty/python/requirements.txt.

So... longer story:

1. In pex clone with patch, edit pex/version.py to use new rev, then run `python2.7 setup.py sdist` - you should now have a new version of pex in dist/ (a tarball).
2. In pants, add the pex dist dir to the [python-setup] repos list as a file url (should end with slash).
3. In pants edit 3rdparty/python/requirements.txt to have an exact requirement for the pex you burned locally, ie: `pex==9.9.9`

The local edit to the pex venv source in pants should continue to be irrelevant.

> If the user doesn't specify a profile, should the default profile automatically be chosen? Or is that what is meant by "no profile configured"?

If no profile is specified in the shell (via `aws-shell --profile` or `.profile`), then we'll fall back to the logic in the AWS CLI, which will first look at `AWS_DEFAULT_PROFILE` before falling back to the `default` profile if it exists.  If there's no default profile we'll start looking at other credential providers for credentials.

> Curious what the user sees if an invalid profile is specified. Let's say 'default' and 'prod' are available but 'beta' is chosen.

There's no validation at the time of switching profiles that the profile does not exist, you'll just get an error when you try to make a CLI call.  The previous PR fixes the issue with crashing if we can't create a client, which would also cover this case.  I think it'd be cool to autocomplete profile names in the future.

Current shell profile changed to: badprofile

aws> iam delete-user --user-name        #<--- no autocomplete happens because we can't create a client for the "badprofile" profile

@tanyunshi -- Just wanted to mention to procedure for updating pull requests. To make changes, you do **not** create a new pull request (most folks seem to do this the first time :). What you do is update the branch you created the pull request with and then push that up to GitHub. The pull request is automatically updated.

That said, in this case, you'll need to create a new pull request because your pull is based on the master branch, and is also based on a different fork (robertdodd/python-docx). So you'll need to get your work into a new branch named 'feature/hyperlink' that's based on the 'feature/hyperlink' branch I've created on python-openxml. If you need help with the rebasing involved, let me know.

There are other challenges with making updates to pull requests, but we can face those when we come to them.

This is a little annoying, but you can do the following:

run `python -m allennlp/run.py train /your/config -s /some/output/path` where your config only contains a `train_dataset_path`. This will save a directory `vocabulary` in `/some/output/path`, which will contain a vocabulary built from only the training data.

The `from_params` [method](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/data/vocabulary.py#L356) in the `Vocabulary` class can handle a directory path to a pre-existing vocabulary. Now you can add your evaluation data and testing data in, along with a `directory_path` key in the `vocabulary` section of your config, meaning you will be using a vocabulary built only from the training data. You can also manually create this vocabulary directory if you need to make other modifications/test other data splits.

Do we want to provide a means to convert PIMS `FramesSequence` objects into dask arrays, (something like `reader.to_da_array()`) or do we want to make the `FramesSequence` an (enhanced) subclass of a dask array by default?

My feeling about this is that the `FramesSequenceND` baseclass ([see here](http://soft-matter.github.io/pims/dev/multidimensional.html#make-your-own-multidimensional-reader)) already provides most of the infrastructure to construct a dask array, as it aware of its own dimensions and allows for registering multiple methods that read blocks of data (in most cases, single frames). We might want to start from there.

BTW "normal" `FramesSequence` objects can be wrapped easily into a `FramesSequenceND`, something I implemented for `pimsviewer` over [here](https://github.com/soft-matter/pimsviewer/blob/master/pimsviewer/utils.py#L99).

Nice! I left some line notes. I am for this, but I have some questions about how we want this to fit into the API.

Some context for @tacaswell and @nkeim: This PR borrows a strategy used by PIMS to carefully guess the dimensionality of the data and represent it appropriately. (This is subtle because of color channels.) For an IPython rich-display repr, this kind of guessing is appropriate. If users are doing something special that confuses the guessing, they can attach their own repr or (more likely) just ignore the repr and plot their data as needed.

But should we extend this guessing to `annotate`? That is, should this `annotate3d`:

* Replace `annotate`, falling back on current behavior for 2D data,
* Exist as `annotate3d`, falling back on `annotate` for 2D data (as currently implemented in this PR)
* Exist as `annotate3d` and complain if data is 2D

I think the last one might be best, though the first one is tempting. I don't think a function called `annotate3d` should fall back on 2D quietly. That seems potentially confusing.

This requires a major rework of the result set class and others. Key idea for the new design:

* `query` allows to get current `result_set`
* A `result_set` is rather basic. It creates the required buffers, binds them to the columns, and implements batched access with something like `next_batch()`. Also provides access to the _raw_ `multi_value_buffers`
* A `row_based_result_set` is based on a `result_set` and takes care of handling the row concept. Grants access to raw memory in terms of `buffer_elements`
* A `cpp_row_based_result_set` is based on a `row_based_result_set` and adds conversion from SQL data type to `turbodbc::field`.

Above components merely shift the responsibilities around without creating new user value. Below components can be added to implement new features:

* A `py_row_based_result_set` is based on `row_based_result_set` and replaces the SQL data type -> `turbodbc::field` -> Python type conversion with a direct hop from SQL data type -> Python type. Should improve performance a little.
* A `numpy_based_result_set` is based on the plain `result_set` and contains specialized routines for copying entire chunks of memory without creating individual C++ or Python objects.

I just successfully tried a patch for the [old `execute_plan`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/3.7.3/conda/plan.py#L537), which is similar to the [current `execute_instructions`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/instructions.py#L116) method.

1. I simply create a list for the failed commands before [line 547](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/3.7.3/conda/plan.py#L547) (`failed_commands = []`)
2. Then add a `try: ... except SystemExit: failed_commands.append((cmd, arg))` around [line 569](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/3.7.3/conda/plan.py#L569) which adds all failed link actions to the `failed_commands` list.
3. Then, just before the end of the method, I simply retry those failed packages: `for cmd, arg in failed_commands: link(prefix, arg, index=index)`.

Works for me, and seems like a pragmatic solution which might solve most of the post-link issues.

Regarding `-dbg` - I use that flag a lot, but just because I use it a lot doesn't mean I want it always. If VUnit can function without it and the user can specify it manually via the public interface, then it should probably be dropped from the core code.

If you think `-dbg` is an important part to `--gui` functionality, then perhaps it could be an extra option included by default. If the user specifies any `vsim` arguments, then they would need to specify `-dbg`. If they don't specify any options, then it would be included by default. In this way there can be some "hand-holding" to get a configuration that works well with the VUnit flow by default, but ultimately the control is given to the user if they want to specify their own flags. Of course, anything that is _required_ for VUnit to work should stay hard-coded as a simulation flag.

> Based on your comment 11th of august, this is not in the scope of this lib. Are you then suggesting another lib building on this, and it's own code, to provide such a feature?

Yes. 

This should be extremely simple to do. Certifi has a public interface that is one function long, that takes no arguments and that returns a string. This is about as simple a module to extend as you can imagine. 

A wrapper module that matches this interface would be almost trivial to implement. Someone doing that would therefore let people have the option of using a more fully-featured solution where that is needed, while keeping this module exactly what it is. 

Again, I should stress the goal of this module. Its only goal is to provide the Mozilla trust bundle in a form suitable for HTTPS. That is *it*. It does not promise to provide a custom trust store for specific use cases, or to be the cross-platform Swiss Army knife of TLS implementations. Those are good things to build, but they are not this. Certifi is one component in such a thing, and we built it. People should feel free to build on top of it.

While many properties of blockchain are great, this one is the most difficult to solve. It means that no personal data can be uploaded to the chain itself, only verifiers of personal data. The best way to link the verifier to the personal data would be ZKPs, simple hashes are considered pseudonymization, not anonymization, even though this opinion is questionable from a technical point of view (it is equivalent to say that good hashing can be backlinked or well encrypted data isn't private, the hacking procedures are  the same...).

The serializables issue perfectly illustrates why this discussion is moot and we should promote more flexible constructs such as `atoms()` instead. If we change `__iter__` to include serializables, then what if a user wants the opposite? We'd have to invent a new method that iterates over `_data` items only. There's no one-size-fits-all behavior for `__iter__`.

Regarding the keys vs. pairs issue, I suppose I could be persuaded, but I have to think more carefully about the full implications. Also, it would almost certainly mean that the mapping mixin would have to be enabled by default (for compatibility reasons), nullifying the efforts to prune the standard API.

Like I said, I'm skeptical of the value of messing with `__iter__` simply because it's inherently limited, and nothing we do can change that. It's not like we can make `iter()` take extra arguments.

from __future__ import division

def fun(W, inds):

   W = W[:,:-1]
   return W[anp.int64(inds)].sum()

W = np.random.randn(5, 10)

g = grad(fun)
print g(W, inds)
```

Mostly I just moved things about a bit.

One significant change I made was to make "Stats" be added to the "allowed_lazy_attributes" of *ALL* Resource instances by default...    I'm not sure how bad of an idea this is.

If a Resource doesn't have stats, then SDK will incorrectly assume it does, try to get it and fail...  probably with a 404.   This violates our policy of preventing known failed operations from ever reaching the wire...   BUT...  it means all Resources that _do_ support stats should automagically work...  "out of the box".

Also I'm not sure about this DottedDict business.   It's definitely cute...   but doesn't it have the potential to confuse the user?   Usually the '.' operator in the SDK implies access of a new URI element?

I think I prefer to force users to access elements of the "stats" object as a dictionary with the '[]' operator.   I think it helps clarify that the 'stats' attribute is a different kind of thing than a resource.


Overall, however, I'm extremely pleased with this feature.

Please don't close it until I've added a few more functional tests.

Please let me know what you think of this version.

I'm not sure what the problem is here.  It is perfectly valid for an API to return no response -- there is a special status code just for that -- 204 No Content.

Now it is true that in this case the service is returning a 200 and an empty response, but since this is effectively "No Content", the SDK returns "None" to signify that.

If your application requires "some response" -- even if it is empty -- you can accomplish this yourself:
```
>>> response = None
>>> whatIneed = response or {}
>>> whatIneed
{}
>>> 
```
it works in python 2.7 but not in 3.6,  I think I know what the problem is, so I'll have a fix soon or an explanation of why my theory was wrong.

I'm afraid there is no good way for your use case at the moment. The best that I can think of is to use a `config_hook` to change the configuration. This function is called after the configuration process, but before the run object is created. Unfortunately, it doesn't have access to the command line options. You would, therefore, have to also use an `option_hook` to read and store the changed name such that the `config_hook` can use that. While possible, this process is utterly ugly and clearly a hack. 
But I can definitely see your point, so would you mind opening a new issue with a feature request?

> The Theano documentation explains quite well that conv2d() will be replaced by other implementations: http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/library/tensor/nnet/conv.html

Yes, that was exactly my error.

The number of parameters of a dense layer equals to `(num_inputs + 1) * num_outputs` (`num_inputs*num_outputs` for the weight matrix, and `num_outputs` for the biases). As you can see from the first table, your first network has an input of `128x35x35` into the first dense layer, so your first dense layer has `(128*35*35 + 1)*500` parameters, that's 78,400,500. That's pretty close to the total number of parameters, since the convolutional layers have comparatively few parameters, and also the extra dense layers only have `501*500` and `501*12`, respectively. Now your attempt at making the network smaller consisted in removing some layers, so the input to the dense layer got *larger*: `32x143x143`. Consequently, the dense layer has even more parameters. To reduce the number of parameters, you need to reduce the size of the input to the first dense layer. Possibly by more pooling, or strided convolution, or global averaging. Maybe also the input of 288x288 is too detailed for a first test and you can downsample it before going into the first convolution.

Closing as it's not an issue with Lasagne. In future, please post such questions on the mailing list first, we can sort it out there and still turn it into a Lasagne issue if you found a bug. Cheers!

`.deb` packages include `postinst` maintainer scripts that are execute as root after installing the package. The current .deb created by electron-builder uses `postinst` to symbolically link nteract to PATH `ln -s /opt/nteract/nteract.sh /usr/bin/nteract`

This script could be used used to add an entry to `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/nteract.list` in order to configure itself for automatic updates via the system update service (assuming updates are distributed using a package archive, not by self-update GitKraken style).

As an Ubuntu user this is the way I would personally perfer things to work. The installaiton flow would be:

1. Download a .deb from the nteract.io website or a github releases page
2. Install the package using the system installer like any other deb packages.
3. The installer `postinst` script adds an entry to `sources.list.d` for nteract
3. The system update service updates my nteract install along with all other software automatically whenever new updates are pushed to the package archive.

Here's a short blurb I wrote up that may be helpful:

KMS encryption and decryption operations allow you to pass an “encryption context”. This is additional data (represented as key-value pairs) that, if supplied during encryption, must also be supplied during decryption.

KMS key grants permit access to KMS keys by AWS IAM users or roles. You can specify operations (e.g. `Decrypt`) and *mandate an encryption context*.

These two features combined let us restrict access to secrets by services.

When you write a secret, you could supply an environment string (e.g. `production` or `development`) and a service name. Then, you could use these values to create the following encryption context and include it in the encryption operation:

`“environment:production,service:service-name”`

These are included in the encryption context for the secret, and are thus tied to the secret.You must provide these exact same context variables (environment and service) when decrypting the secret.

You would then create a key grant for the production service user could specify that they can only use the KMS credstash key for decryption if they send the `“environment:production,service:service-name”` encryption context:

`aws kms create-grant --key-id $STEALTH_KEY --grantee-principal "arn:aws:iam::0000000000:role/service-name" --constraints "EncryptionContextEquals={environment=production,service=service-name}" --operations Decrypt`

This way, the service user/role could only get access to the secrets they are intended to get access to. They couldn’t decrypt the secrets for a different service, or a different environment, because they wouldn’t have access to decrypting with that encryption context.

Yeah, not entirely surprising. (SendGrid's v3 API actively prevents multiple reply-to addresses, so it doesn't seem likely they'd handle multiple from emails.)

You got me curious, though, and I ran some quick tests to see which ESPs would even support the `from_email='first@example.com, second@example.com'` form:

* Mailgun allows multiple from fields
* Mandrill's API doesn't have any way to express more than one from
* Postmark accepts multiple addresses in its From field, but silently truncates to just the first one before sending
* SendGrid's v3 API doesn't have any way to express more than one from
* SparkPost allows a comma-separated from string

**But:** even if you switched to an ESP that supports them, you'd probably run into deliverability issues with multiple from addresses. Gmail flat-out rejects the message ("5.7.1 Messages with multiple addresses in From: header are not accepted.  - gsmtp"). Outlook.com accepts the message, but seems to consider multiple from addresses a spam signal (my tests ended up in the junk folder).

For completeness, I think I'm going to change Anymail to support a multi-address from_email string for Mailgun and SparkPost, and to issue a clearer "unsupported" error for the other ESPs. But in practice, multiple from addresses probably aren't all that usable.

For your project, one approach I've seen with forwarding is encoding one email address in another. E.g.: `From: support+customer%example.com@company.com`. (Assuming company.com's mail supports +-aliases, that delivers to `support@company.com`, but you can still extract the "other from" from the address.)

The approach seems sound to me, but it would be really good to avoid a `sizeof(PyObject *)` size increase for every instance. As far as I've gathered from the issue thread, this only affects nurses which have `Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_GC` -- in pybind11 this only applies to types which have a `__dict__`. So I'd suggest the following alternative:

* Stash `patients` inside the instance's `__dict__` (if it has one; if not, it doesn't participate in GC anyway).
* Modify `pybind11_clear` to avoid clearing patients. E.g. pop them from the dictionary, `Py_CLEAR(dict)` and then restore the dict and patients.
* Nothing extra needs to be done in `clear_instance` since the patience should be cleared away with the dict (after the C++ object, as required).

All of the assumptions in code here are based on things tested manually.

That is:

- If python is compiled without IPv6, binding to an IPv6 port will raise `socket.error`.
- On FreeBSD, IPv4 traffic will not forward to a socket bound on an IPv6 port, but you can ask for two different servers on the same address/port, with different protocols.
- On Ubuntu, IPv4 traffic will forward to a socket bound on an IPv6 port, but you cannot ask for two different servers on the same address/port, with different protocols.

Regarding the missing ``FuzzyChoice`` equivalent in Faker, you can avoid having to create new lists of valid choices by adding a new provider to the bundled ``Faker``:


```
# somefile.py
class ChoiceProvider(BaseProvider):
    def random_choice(self, choices=None):
        """
        Given a list of choices in Django format, return a random value
        """
        choice = self.random_element(choices)
        return choice[0]
```

You then have to add the new provider:

```
from somefile import ChoiceProvider
factory.Faker.add_provider(ChoiceProvider)
```

Once the new provider has been added, use it like this:

```
class MyFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    choice_field = factory.Faker('random_choice', choices=mymodel.CHOICE_FIELD_CHOICES)
```

Having to include the ``add_provider`` boilerplate in all your factory files is a bit sub-optimal.
Personally, I feel it is a simple enough class that it could be added directly to factory boy instead of submitted as a PR to faker. I'm just not sure that it provides any value to faker outside the context of a django project using factory boy, so they'd be hesitant to accept it.  I'd be happy to submit a PR here if anyone would like.

I think we have to make choices:

Options group A:
1. Change Arrow (change its plasma client)
2. Change Ray (borrow plasma client from Arrow and change it)

Options group B:
1.  Change C++ codes, implement event loops in C++, export async callbacks to Python via `Cython` (harder to make use of asyncio, higher performance)
2. Change C++ codes (keep parts without I/O), use Python to write parts related to I/O with with `asyncio`'s event loops (normal performance)
3. Just change Python codes (may hurt performance but easier to implement)

Options group C:
1. Keep both async & blocking implementation (higher performance, more codes)
2. Make everything async, create an event loop and wait for every blocking API
3. Make everything async, keep an event loop for each worker and used by every blocking API

Currently I prefer A2, B2, C3

So the idea is to implement example that likes your timeline feed in infinity cycle? 
OK.

So I'm basically processing events that are being fed from one system, doing some processing on them, then sending them off to another system. The problem is when I send them to this other system, I need to send them in batches (because the number of incoming invents is a reasonably significant number, and this other system doesn't want that many single API calls, it wants them batched up).

So I have one task that is accepting incoming events, processing them, and then throwing them into a ``trio.Queue``, and then other tasks are pulling items off of that queue, until it hits the batch size, and then triggers the other API call.

I can do something like:

```python
async def sender(q):
    to_send = []
    while True:
        with trio.move_on_after(30):
            to_send.append(await q.get())
            if len(to_send) < 10000:
                continue

        # TODO: Do the actual sending logic
        to_send = []
```

That works, though it introduces a second queue (but at least this second queue is bounded still), but the big problem with this, is if you have multiple instances of ``sender`` running at once, all acting on the same ``trio.Queue``, then you end up buffering up to N per task, instead of just up to N.

Another option to get around it is to instead of having the sending tasks directly processing the ``trio.Queue``, have a single collector pulling single items off of the queue, and locally buffering them until it gets to the maximum size of a batch it wants (or enough time passes) and then sending the whole batch into a second ``trio.Queue``. That also works, but I think it would be a lot nicer if this could be baked into trio itself, to enable something like:

```python
async def sender(q):
    while True:
        with trio.move_on_after(30):
            to_send = await q.get_batch(10000)
            # TODO: Do the actual sending logic.
```

I think that provides a much cleaner API than the other options provide.

Systemd documentation for well-behaved Linux daemons: https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/daemon.html#New-Style%20Daemons

(See also: [suggestions for writing daemons that accept sockets from systemd or fall back to creating their own](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/daemon.html#Porting%20Existing%20Daemons))

MacOS documentation for well-behaved MacOS daemons: https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/MacOSX/Conceptual/BPSystemStartup/Chapters/CreatingLaunchdJobs.html

That MacOS docs page says that daemons should "check in" (which according to the sample code uses `launch_msg`) and get the "launch dictionary", but I guess actually this [is deprecated](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xpc/xpc_functions?language=objc)?

> I thought it was a sort of bidirectional link: for the task to be able to send a "cancel" to a cancel scope and for the cancel scope to notify the cancellation (in-process) to the task. According to your comments, it is only for the "Cancel scope to task" notification means.

Oh, I see! Yeah, by "cancel binding" I just mean, like "a specific `with cancel_scope` statement". If a single cancel scope can be used with multiple `with`s, then it's useful to be able to talk about those as individuals – e.g. they might catch different exceptions.

> but I don't see a problem in letting the caller send to the cancel scope some information about him or about the cancellation "reason" when he invokes the cancel operation on the cancel scope. This is seeing the cancel operation as a method of the cancel scope that can be explicitly called.

When initially designing cancel scopes, I considered trying to track the "reason" for a cancellation (e.g., timeout versus someone calling `.cancel`), or passing extra state like an exception type to inject. This gets really tricky, though, because: what if `.cancel` gets called twice with different reasons, oh *and also* the timeout expired? And everyone wants to use a different exception type? It's a mess. So instead, we treat cancellation as a simple boolean state, and that acts as a barrier between the mechanisms for mutating that state versus the mechanisms for notifying about that state.

That said, it would be possible to expand from a single boolean to like... multiple booleans. (The "tagged boolean" idea.)

> Although I don't understand your final: "though you still can't select on source of the cancellation". What do you mean?

I mean you could say "I only care about cancel scopes tagged with BLAH", but not, "I only care about cancels from from cancel scope object X, not cancel scope object Y".

> And second, I insist in not trying to desperately simplify the overall design too soon. It would only deter creativity. We will have time for that. Let's give us some more time to find examples and situations where it may be useful. I neither have them yet, but let the river flow... and we'll see where it takes us.

Sure, that kind of low-stakes exploring ideas is exactly what this kind of issue is for. But I'm just noting that this kind of complexity will need to find some pretty compelling use cases before it can graduate from "let the river flow" to something we actually do. Cancellation is inherently a viciously complicated thing to understand and use. Trio works *really* hard to make it as simple as possible, and it's still far from trivial. So when brainstorming my main goal is to find ideas that will make it easier to understand or solve common practical problems.
